CITY OF ORANGE

CITY ATTORNEY—(714) 532-0351                                    FAX (714) 532-0437

Peggy Bernardi
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Re:  REQUEST FOR INFORMAL WRITTEN ADVICE

Dear Ms. Bernardi:

     Pursuant to our conversation with you and John Rothman, I am
enclosing two additional documents for your review clarifying my
letter for informal written advice over a potential conflict of
interest created by a bond counsel and redevelopment agency counsel
being one in the same.

     Attached you will find a comprehensive opinion of the law firm
of Kane, Ballmer & Berkman opining that such a conflict exists.
This material is offered as background to assist you in your
research.  Also attached is a survey conducted by the undersigned
of over 90 redevelopment agencies in the State of California
regarding dual status counsel.  This material is also offered as
background to provide an overview of the current practices in the
State regarding this situation.

     I look forward to your assistance and advice in this important
matter for the City of Orange

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        Luis A. Rodriguez
                                        Sr. Assistant City Attorney

ORANGE CIVIC CENTER  •  300 E. CHAPMAN AVE.  •  ORANGE, CA 92666-1591  •  P.O. BOX 449

KANE, BALLMER & BERKMAN
A LAW CORPORATION

354 SOUTH SPRING STREET, SUITE 420
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90013

TELEPHONE (213) 617-0480
MURRAY O. KANE
TELECOPIER (213) 625-0931                    ROBERT P. BERKMAN
BRUCE C. BALLMER                              RETIRED
GLENN F. WASSERMAN
MARJORIE R. FRIEDLANDER
R. BRUCE TEPPER, JR.
RENÉE L. CAMPBELL                             EUGENE B. JACOBS
KATHRYN REIMANN                               A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
PRINCIPALS                                    OF COUNSEL


                              December 7, 1990


                                                         By Hand Delivery


Mr. Luis A. Rodriguez
Senior Assistant City Attorney and
Agency Counsel
Orange Redevelopment Agency
300 East Chapman Avenue
Orange, California 92666

     SUBJECT:   The Application of Conflict of Interest Laws to
                Bond Counsel Contingent Fee Arrangements and
                Participation of the Same Firm As Special Counsel

Dear Mr. Rodriguez:

     You have requested that we assess and advise you on the
possibility that a firm which has served as special counsel to
the Orange Redevelopment Agency for a number of years may have a
conflict of interest in representing the Agency as bond counsel at
a well where its compensation as bond counsel is based in part on the
size of the bond issue.  In short, could its advice to the Agency
on Agency contracts and other matters be improperly influenced by
an inherent advantage to issuing certain kinds or amounts of bonds
or other financing instruments? While not a common practice in the
field, we are aware that such a dual role may be performed by a few
other firms as well.

     We understand that you may be seeking a formal opinion from
the Fair Political Practices Commission or Attorney General, as
appropriate, in view of the importance to the Agency of determining
whether it should use special counsel and bond counsel in dual
roles in the future. In addition, it seems important to get an
answer to this question in a way which will allow you to determine
whether any of the Agency contracts and/or decisions in which the
special counsel participated, which occurred in the past, might be
subject to question or how they might best be protected if
necessary.

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 2


     Finally, if there is a question about the validity of the
Agency's past practice, a concern arises as to the validity of the
special counsel and/or bond counsel agreements themselves [includ-
ing the status of the proposed but unexecuted agreement for the
recent $4,000,000 Northwest Redevelopment Project, Subordinated
Taxable Tax Allocation Notes, Issue of 1990] and the appropriate-
ness of paying [or not] and/or recovering fees thereunder.

     As discussed below, the question of the appropriateness of
agency special counsel being paid for its services on the basis of
a formula tied to increases in the value of property in the
applicable jurisdiction which might be affected by its advice was
answered negatively by the California Attorney General in 1983 in
66 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 376 (the "1983 OCAG"). Given the continuing
concern in the State for limiting conflict situations, it is
possible that the conclusion in the 1983 OCAG, with other legal
authority, would now be applied to invalidate bond counsel contin-
gent fee arrangements where broader advice is given as special
counsel.

     Our assessment of that question is set forth below.

                              I.
                            FACTS

     The firm of Stradling, Yocca, Carlson & Rauth (the "Firm") has
served as special counsel to the Orange Redevelopment Agency (the
"Agency"). As special counsel, the Firm among other things may
have given advice to, negotiated on behalf of, and otherwise
represented the Agency with respect to cooperation agreements,
disposition and development agreements, owner participation agree-
ments and other land use contracts the Agency has entered into with
other governmental entities or owners or developers of land
("Redevelopment Contracts").

     From time to time, the Agency in the past has and in the
future will issue securities such as bonds, notes or certificates
of participation [hereinafter, the term "bonds" shall mean any of
such types of securities] to finance its activities within redevel-
opment project areas. Such financings are used to provide for the
financing of redevelopment projects or particular developments.
Such financings consist of tax allocation bond financings and
revenue bond financings. Tax allocation bond financings are
financings payable from a portion of the property taxes collected
from within a project area based upon the increase in assessed
valuation of land, improvements and public utility property, and
revenue bond financings are financings payable from revenue
generated from a particular development.

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 3


        We have been informed that the bonds the Agency has issued
since 1986 consist of the following:

        a.    $4,000,000 Orange Redevelopment Agency, Tustin Street
              Redevelopment Project, Tax Allocation Bonds, Issue of
              1986.

        b.    $16,500,000 Orange Redevelopment Agency, Southwest
              Redevelopment Project, As Amended, Tax Allocation Bonds,
              Issue of 1986.

        c.    $2,500,000 Orange Redevelopment Agency, California,
              Variable Rate Demand Multifamily Housing Revenue Bonds,
              (Mercury Savings and Loan Association/Palmyra Seniors
              Apartments), 1987 Series A.

        d.    $10,000,000 Orange Redevelopment Agency, Southwest
              Redevelopment Project, Taxable Tax Allocation Notes,
              Issue of 1988.

        e.    $5,000,000 Orange Redevelopment Agency, Northwest
              Redevelopment Project, Taxable Tax Allocation Notes,
              Issue of 1988.

        f.    $10,000,000 Orange Redevelopment Agency, Southwest
              Redevelopment Project, Parity Subordinated Tax Allocation
              Notes, Issue of 1988.

        g.    $8,000,000 Orange Redevelopment Agency, Northwest
              Redevelopment Project, Subordinated Tax Allocation Notes,
              Issue of 1988.

        h.    $14,845,000 City of Orange Police Facility Headquarters
              Project Certificates of Participation issued in 1989.

        i.    $7,000,000 Orange Redevelopment Agency, Tustin Street
              Redevelopment    Project,    Subordinated    Taxable    Tax
              Allocation Notes, Issue of 1990.

        j.    $4,000,000 Orange Redevelopment Agency, Northwest
              Redevelopment    Project,    Subordinated    Taxable    Tax
              Allocation Notes, Issue of 1990.

        We are not aware of the bond issues except item "j" above, for
which the firm acted as bond counsel. Nor have we investigated or
been informed of which Redevelopment Contracts of the Agency, if
any, could have been financed with, provided revenues for debt
service for, or otherwise affected any of the bond issues. There-
fore, the discussion below is necessarily based on general princi-

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 4


ples and the practices in redevelopment, including those of special
counsel and bond counsel.

     The one bond issue for which we have been provided a proposed
bond counsel fee agreement (the "Fee Agreement") is for the
$4,000,000 Northwest Redevelopment Project, Subordinated Taxable
Tax Allocation Notes, Issue of 1990 (item "j" above), referred to
as the "Northwest Notes". Apparently, the Fee Agreement for the
Firm's services as bond counsel never was approved by the Agency or
entered into prior to or at the time the Northwest Notes were
issued. Also, we understand that the Firm has still not been paid
for services it rendered as bond counsel in connection with the
Northwest Notes. The Firm has asked the Agency to approve and
execute the Fee Agreement. The Fee Agreement would consist of a
letter from the Firm, dated September 18, 1990, and which the City
of Orange (the "City") or the Agency would countersign. The Fee
Agreement would provide that the amount of fee that would be paid
to the Firm would be based upon the total principal amount of each
issue of bonds authorized and sold. It also would state as
follows:

     In the event a financing is not completed for
     any reason, the agency and the City will have
     no liability for any fees incurred by our firm
     with respect to the financing.

     The Firm would be reimbursed for out-of-pocket expenses. The Fee
Agreement also would provide that the Firm would be employed on the
same terms to act as bond counsel in connection with all other and
additional note, bond and certificate of participation issues with
respect to the redevelopment projects of the Agency which occur
during the 1990-91 fiscal year.

     The Firm has indicated that in its capacity of special
counsel, it has not taken part in the selection by the Agency of
whom it will use as bond counsel.

     We assume that, as with the case of a typical bond counsel,
the Firm as bond counsel for the Northwest Notes, participated in
the discussion, structuring, negotiation and drafting of bond
financing documents to which the Agency was a party such as bond
indentures, trust agreements, bond purchase contracts, and reim-
bursement agreements. In addition, we assume the Firm participated
in structuring the financing.

     It is possible that Redevelopment Contracts the Firm has
participated in preparing as special counsel may have resulted or
will result in work being done within the Northwest Redevelopment
Project which has led to or will lead to an increase in the

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 5


assessed valuation of property. Since the tax allocation bonds are
payable from taxes generated by the increase in assessed valuation
of property, the total principal amount of the tax allocation bond
financing may depend, in part, on the size of the potential
increase in that assessed valuation. Also, the structure of the
Redevelopment Contracts and the transactions upon which they are
based may influence whether they should be financed with bonds or
some other method, as well as the characteristics of the financing
which might affect its size, such as whether it would be funded
interest, whether it would be tax exempt or taxable, whether
it would be certificates of participation or bonds, what the debt
service coverage and/or other security would be, and whether the
bonds would be subject to public bidding or negotiated sale.
Therefore, it is possible that such Redevelopment Contracts may
result or have resulted in an increase in the total principal
amount of the bond financing.

In addition to participating in the preparation of Redevel-
opment Contracts, the special counsel to the Agency may be involved
in rendering advice to the Agency regarding matters related to the
issuance of bonds. While bond counsel would be retained by the
Agency to provide services to the Agency with respect to the
issuance of bonds, it often would have interests not necessarily
corresponding in important respects to those of the Agency. A
principal function would be to render an opinion with respect to
the bonds. In providing services, bond counsel often must consider
the impact that advice it gives may have on future bondholders.
Special counsel would generally represent the broader interests of
the Agency. While bond counsel will often stress maximizing the
security for repayment and marketability of the bonds, special
counsel would commonly weigh other factors as well to ensure that
the Agency's transactions are facilitated. Bond counsel may be
inclined to maximize coverage ratios, maximize the period for the
escrow of funds and maximize the security interests to secure the
repayment of the bonds. Special counsel often seeks legal ways to
have as much of the bond proceeds available as early as possible
for the Agency's use and to minimize the burden on the Agency.
Many of these countervailing considerations can affect the amount
and timing of bond issues and thus bond counsel fees based thereon.

It should be noted that eight of the ten bond issues listed
above are tax allocation bonds. Such bonds were issued for each of
the three redevelopment projects in the City [Mustel Street, South-
west and Northwest]. In addition the Police Facility Headquarters
Certificates of Participation appear to have a reimbursement agree-
ment for tax increments to be paid from the Agency to the City in
connection with the financing. The one remaining bond issue is
based on housing revenues. Thus, the analysis contained herein
for the Northwest Notes, would apply likewise to most of the other

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 6


bond issues, if the Firm acted as bond counsel for them under a
similar contingent fee contract.

II.
ANALYSIS

A. ASSESSMENT UNDER GOVERNMENT CODE §§ 1090, ET SEQ.

1. Is the Firm Financially Interested in
Redevelopment Contracts Which It Participated
in Making?

a. Applicable Statute

Government Code § 1090 sets forth a prohibition against
conflicts of interest by certain government officers and employees.
Government Code § 1090 provides in part as follows:

. . . district . . . and city officers or employees shall
not be financially interested in any contract made by
them in their official capacity, or by any body or board
of which they are members. Nor shall . . . district
. . . and city officers or employees be purchasers at any
sale or vendors at any purchase made by them in their official
capacity.

As used in this article, "district" means any agency of
the state formed pursuant to general law or special act,
for the local performance of governmental or proprietary
functions within limited boundaries.

b. Application to the Agency and Firm

The Agency is an agency formed pursuant to the California
Community Redevelopment Law. As such, the Agency is an agency of
the state for purposes of § 1090.

The Firm acting as special counsel to the Agency, would be
considered an officer of the Agency for purposes of § 1090. The
California Supreme Court in People ex rel. Clancy v. Superior
Court, 39 Cal.3d 740, 743, 747, 218 Cal.Rptr. 24, 25, 28 (1985),
cert.denied, 475 U.S. 1121, 93 L.Ed.2d 108 (1986), held that an
attorney retained by a city as a special attorney solely for the
purpose of bringing abatement actions under a new ordinance was a
public officer even though the retainer agreement provided that the
attorney was to be an independent contractor and not an officer or
employee of the city. Although the Supreme Court held that the
special attorney was a public officer in order to apply to the
officer a provision of the ABA Code of Professional Responsibility

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 7


which applied to lawyers who were public officers, the Court did
not limit its holding to the context of the ABA Code.  Also, in
Schaefer v. Berinstein, 140 Cal.App.2d 278, 295 P.2d 113 (1956)
the court held that an attorney hired by a city as a special
attorney for the purpose of rehabilitating tax-deeded and special
assessment frozen properties situated within the city was an
officer for purposes of § 1090.  140 Cal.App.2d at 291, 295 P.2d at
122.

     The meaning of the term "made" as used in § 1090 is clarified
in Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae, 262
Cal.App.2d 222, 237, 69 Cal.Rptr. 251, 262 P(1968).  In Millbrae,
the court stated that although § 1090 referred to a contract "made"
by an officer or employee, the word "made" was not used in the
statute in its narrower and technical contract sense but was used
in the broad sense to encompass such embodiments in the making of
a contract as preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises,
reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and solicitations and
solicitations for bids.  Therefore, if it was found in the case
here that the Firm as special counsel had such involvement in the
preparation of Redevelopment Contracts, then such Redevelopment
Contracts would be deemed to have been "made" by the Firm in its
official capacity for purposes of § 1090.

     C.     Financial Interest of the Firm

     It is assumed that neither the Firm nor any of its attorneys
are parties to any of the Redevelopment Contracts which could
affect the size of the subject bond issue.  The central question
then becomes, does the Firm's contingent fee on bond counsel agree-
ment, where the amount of fees paid could conceivably be affected
by the Redevelopment Contracts, give the Firm such an indirect
"financial interest" in the Redevelopment Contracts as to violate
§ 1090.

     (1)  Attorney General Opinion.  The California Attorney
General has addressed the question of whether § 1090 would be
violated in a fact situation similar to the case here.

     In the "1983 OCAG", the Attorney General opined that if a city
entered into an employment contract whereby it hired a city
attorney and compensated such attorney with a percentage of the
increase in assessed value of parcels of property within the city's
boundaries, official involvement of such a person in contracts
which might increase the assessed value of such parcels would
violate § 1090.  Applying the 1983 OCAG analysis to the case here,
§ 1090 would be violated if the Agency and the Firm entered into
the Fee Agreement and the Firm, as special counsel to the Agency,
participated in making redevelopment Contracts which might increase

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 8


directly or indirectly the total principal amount of bonds to be
issued or determine whether or not bonds would be issued in any
bond financing in which the Firm served as bond counsel pursuant to
the Fee Agreement.

In the 1983 OCAG, the Attorney General pointed out that a city
attorney would, with rare exceptions, be a participant in the
making of land contracts between the city and the city's agencies
and between the city and private landowners and developers. The
Attorney General went on to point out that the ordinarily contemplated and foreseeable result of a contract between a city and its
redevelopment agency, on the one hand, and a landowner or a developer, on the other hand, or between the city and the agency itself,
would be an improvement in the parcel. Then obviously the city
attorney, under the proposed employment contract in which compensation was to be based on a percentage of the increase in assessed
value of parcels of property within the city's boundaries, would
have a financial interest in contracts that might generate tax base
increases and particularly in those that might cause large
increases. 66 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. at 379, 380.

In the case here, the Firm as special counsel may participate
in the making of Redevelopment Contracts such as cooperation
agreements, disposition and development agreements, owner participation agreements and other land use contracts between the Agency
and the city, other governmental entities, developers or landowners. It is an "ordinarily contemplated and foreseeable result
of many such Redevelopment Contracts that they will generate
increases in the assessed value of parcels of property and also a
greater need for cash flow to fund projects within a project area.
An increase in the assessed value of parcels of property and the
greater need for cash flow could likely result in an increase in
the principal amount of bonds in tax allocation bond financings.
If so, then the Firm would have a financial interest in such
Redevelopment Contracts if the Firm was also serving as bond
counsel in a related tax allocation bond financing pursuant to the
Fee Agreement. The Firm's financial interest in the Redevelopment
Contracts would be similar to the city attorney's financial interest
in the contracts set forth in the 1983 OCAG. The Firm's
participation in making such Redevelopment Contracts which would
constitute a violation of § 1090. As discussed below, the Attorney
General's opinion referred to above has extended the application of
§ 1090 to a more remote financial interest than found in the court
cases, but which nevertheless seems consistent with the language
and policy of the statute, and which could be an extrapolation from
cases where an indirect financial interest was found to constitute
a conflict.

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 9


     (2)  California Cases.  A large number of cases have involved
conflicts where an officer either directly entered into or had an
ownership interest in an entity which entered into a contract with
the governmental entity of which he or she was an officer.  In
Osburn v. Stone, 170 Cal.480, 150 P. 367 (1915), the court in
reversing a demurrer held that money paid to a law partnership
pursuant to a contract for the services of such law partnership was
illegal where a member of the city's board of education was a
member of the law partnership.  170 Cal.at 490, 491; 150 P. at 372.

     In a more recent Supreme Court case, Thomson v. Call, 38
Cal.3d 633, 214 Cal.Rptr. 139 (1985), cert.denied, 474 U.S. 1057,
88 L.Ed.2d 773 (1986), a developer, in order to obtain from the
city a use permit for property it owned, agreed to a plan with the
city, pursuant to which the developer was required to acquire
certain land and convey it to the city for use as a park.  A city
councilman owned a parcel of land located within this targeted
area.  The plan required the developer to allocate $600,000 of its
own money for the acquisition of the land for the park; to spend
that sum or less, for the acquisition of such land by the developer
through "private negotiation"; to convey any land so acquired to
the city for use as part of the park; and to pay to the city any
unexpended balance of the $600,000 for use in "condemning by
eminence domain" any land required for the park but not acquired
from the developer pursuant to the plan.  The developer negotiated
with the councilman and acquired his property for $258,000.  The
developer then conveyed the property to the city.

     The Court held that however complex the transaction, the
developer's purchase of property from the councilman and its
conveyance of that property to the city, were in performance of a
single multi-party agreement.  It indicated that the scope of this
agreement and the interdependency of the many subordinate trans-
actions were best revealed in the escrow instructions themselves.
In response to the councilman's argument that the purchase of his
parcel was not a term or condition of the contract between the
developer and the city, the Court replied that the prospect that
performance of the contract would involve acquisition of the
councilman's land and conveyance of that land to the city was
contemplated by all parties.  Purchase of the councilman's parcel
was clearly part of a previous arrangement; the fact that the real
estate purchase contract between the councilman and the developer
was entered into after the city's acceptance of the plan in no way
altered the fact that the transactions between the councilman, the
developer and the city were part of a single agreement.  38 Cal.3d
at 644, 214 Cal.Rptr. at 145.

     The Court indicated that California courts have consistently
voided contracts where the public officer was found to have any

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 10


direct or indirect interest in a contract.  Here, the Court found
that as part of the transaction at issue, the councilman sold
property to the city, using the developer as a conduit.

     Whether we regard his interest as direct or indirect, it
is clearly a pecuniary interest forbidden by section 1090
and by the decisions applying conflict-of-interest rules
generally.  Neither the absence of actual fraud nor the
possibility of a 'good faith' mistake on [the councilman's] part can affect the conclusion that this contract
violates section 1090 and is therefore void.

38 Cal.3d at 646, 214 Cal.Rptr. at 146.

     If Thomson were applied to the case here, the parties with
whom the Agency entered into Redevelopment Contracts, which the
Firm participated as special counsel in making, would be viewed as
mere conduits, which the Firm used to increase the fee it would
receive under the Fee Agreement.  However, contracts the Firm
participated as special counsel in making would less likely be
viewed as mere conduits than the councilman's transaction with the
developer in Thomson.  It is more likely that development
purposes would have been a stronger element in the Agency trans-
actions.  This clearly was not the case in Thomson.  In Thomson,
the developer was required to spend $600,000 either in acquiring
property through negotiated sale or by giving it to the city for
use in acquiring property.  It was as though the $600,000 was the
developer's fee to the city for the permit it was seeking and the
city authorized the developer to use the money to acquire the
property.

     As pointed out by the Court in Thomson, the prospect that
performance of the contract between the city and the developer
would involve acquisition of the land and conveyance of that land to the city was contemplated by all parties since the
land was one of only three parcels which were expressly targeted in
the contract for acquisition.  The only purpose for the agreement
between the city and developer was for the developer to acquire
such land.

     In the case here, it is unlikely that the Redevelopment
Contracts were entered into by the Agency only to increase the
property value within a project area and the amount of bonds to be
issued, and thus with the intent that they directly increase the
fees to be paid pursuant to the Fee Agreement.  Nevertheless,
§ 1090 may be violated even if the specific or principal purpose is
not to increase the bond counsel fees, but it is a foreseeable
result and the Redevelopment Contracts could be influenced by such
a possibility.

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 11


Stigall v. City of Taft, 58 Cal.2d 565, 25 Cal.Rptr. 441
(1962), was a Supreme Court case which applied § 1090 to this sort
of possible indirect influence. In Stigall, a city councilman who
was in charge of the council's building committee also was the
owner of approximately three percent of the shares of stock of a
plumbing company, which was one of the low bidders for a city
contract. The court overruled the demurrer to an action to declare
the contract with the plumbing company void.

Although the facts of Stigall are distinguishable, the Court
sets forth language as to how § 1090 should be applied. The Court
stated in its analysis as follows:

The instant statutes are concerned with any interest,
other than perhaps a remote or minimal interest, which
would prevent the officials involved from exercising
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance to the best
interests of the city.

58 Cal.2d at 569, 25 Cal.Rptr. at 443.

Also, the Court, quoting U.S. v. Mississippi Valley Co., 364
U.S. 520, 549-550, 5 L.Ed.2d 268, stated,

The statute is thus directed not only at dishonor, but
also at conduct that tempts dishonor. This broad
proscription embodies a recognition of the fact that an
impairment of impartial judgment can occur in even the
most well-meaning men when their personal economic
interests are affected by the business they transact on
behalf of the Government. To this extent, therefore, the
statute is more concerned with what might have happened
in a given situation than with what actually happened.
It attempts to prevent honest government agents from
succumbing to temptation by making it illegal for them to
enter into relationships which are fraught with
temptation.

58 Cal.2d at 570, 25 Cal.Rptr. at 444.

Applying this principle to the case here, the Firm, if it were
to enter the Agreement, arguably would have an interest in which
could prevent it from exercising absolute loyalty and
undivided allegiance to the best interests of the Agency in making
related Redevelopment Contracts.

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 12


       d.    Summary

       As indicated above, California cases in which a § 1090
violation has been found mostly involved situations in which an
officer or employee either directly entered into a contract with a
governmental entity of which he or she was an officer or employee
or had an ownership interest in a third party which in turn entered
into a contract with the governmental entity of which he or she was
an officer or employee.  Also involved were cases such as Thomson
in which the officer entered into an agreement with a third party
which in turn entered into an agreement with the governmental
entity where it was contemplated that the officer would enter into
an agreement with the third party.

       The 1983 OCAG differs from such California cases in that it
does not involve a commercial transaction directly or indirectly
between the officer or employee and the governmental entity but
rather involves how the officer's conduct during the performance of
his duties may impact the fees he receives. The financial interest
of the officer in the contracts set forth in the 1983 OCAG is more
attenuated than the financial interest an officer would have if the
officer participated in making a contract with an entity in which
he or she had a financial or ownership interest as is the case in
the California cases cited.

       In the case here, the Firm's financial interest in the
Redevelopment Contracts would be similar to the financial interest
the city attorney in the 1983 OCAG had in the contracts he was to
participate in making because the Firm's compensation would depend
on the principal amount of bonds issued which in turn could be
influenced by increases in the value of property brought about by
the Redevelopment Contracts. The principal amount of bonds issued
could also be affected by other characteristics of a financing,
which could in turn be influenced by the structure of the Redevel-
opment Contracts and the transactions upon which they are based.

       2.    What is the Consequence of a Financial
             Interest in Violation of § 1090?

       Government Code § 1092 provides in pertinent part as follows:

       [e]very contract made in violation of any of the
       provisions of Section 1090 may be avoided at the instance
       of any party except the officer interested therein.

       In Thomson, the Court, after holding that the transactions
involved were in the nature of a single multi-party agreement and
that such agreement violated § 1090, held that the agreement there-
fore was void.  The Court did not distinguish the city-developer

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 13


agreement from the developer-councilman agreement in evaluating the
entire transaction and the appropriate remedy.  The Court allowed
the city to retain the land which the councilman had sold to the
developer and which the developer in turn conveyed to the city and
also allowed the city to recover the $258,000 paid by the developer
to the councilman for the land plus interest.  In addition the
Court stated

     Clearly, no recovery could be had for goods delivered or
services rendered to the city or public agency pursuant
to a contract violative of section 1090 or similar
conflict-of-interest statutes. . . Moreover, the city
or agency is entitled to recover any consideration which
it has paid, without restoring the benefits received
under the contract.

38 Cal.3d at 646, 647, 214 Cal.Rptr. at 146, 147.

     The Thomson Court indicated that California courts generally
have held that a contract in which a public officer was interested
was void per se and not merely voidable.  38 Cal.3d at 646, 214 Cal.Rptr. at
146.  One of the cases it cited was Berka v. Woodward, 125 Cal.
119, 57 P. 777 (1899), in which the Court held that a contract
pursuant to which a person who was an officer of a city and a
member of its common council supplied lumber and materials to the
city was void.  The Berka Court stated,

     . . . when a contract is expressly prohibited by law, no
court of justice will entertain an action upon it, or
upon any asserted rights growing out of it.

125 Cal. at 126, 57 P. at 779.

     Quoting from Swanger v. Mayberry, 59 Cal. 91, 93, the Berka Court
further stated,

     The general principle is well established that a contract
founded on an illegal consideration, or which is made for
the purpose of furthering any matter or thing prohibited
by statute, or to aid or assist any party therein, is
void. . . . The rule, further, is that, where a statute
pronounces a penalty for an act, a contract founded on
such act is void, although the statute does not pronounce
it void nor expressly prohibit it.

     The Thomson court went on to quote Co. of Shasta v. Moody, 90
Cal.App. 519, 265 P. 1032 (1928), stating:

Luis A. Rodriguez

     . . . because the contracts were void under the statute
and against public policy, "there is no ground for any
equitable considerations, presumptions, or estoppels.
. . . The contracts in the case at bar, as we have seen, are
against the express prohibition of the law and courts will not
entertain any rights growing out of such a contract, or permit
a recovery upon a quantum meruit or quantum valeant."

38 Cal.3d at 647, 214 Cal.Rptr. at 147.

the Redevelopment Contracts may be considered to have been made in
violation of § 1090, because these would be the contracts in which
the Firm was financially interested and which would have been made
by the Firm in its official capacity. However, the Fee Agreement
also may be considered to have been made in violation of § 1090,
because without entering into the Fee Agreement, no violation of
§ 1090 would occur. It could be argued that because § 1092 states
that such a contract "may be avoided at the instance of any part
except the officer interested therein," the contract referred to in
§ 1092 must necessarily have the officer either as a party to the
contract or else have an ownership or other indirect interest in
the entity which is a party to the contract.

     Furthermore, if the Redevelopment Contracts themselves were to
be avoidable pursuant to § 1092 or were to be void under Thomson,
this result could render hardship on either the Agency or the
entity with whom the Agency had contracted. Such a result also
would not appear to promote the goal of prohibiting interests of
officers which could prevent such officer from exercising absolute
loyalty and undivided allegiance to the best interests of the
Agency. See Stigall, 58 Cal.2d at 569, 25 Cal.Rptr. at 443. In
addition, in no California case involving a § 1090 violation has
the court ruled in a manner which has resulted in hardship to the
governmental entity or an innocent third party. A more likely and
intended result would be that the Fee Agreement was void and the
Firm would have to return all compensation received pursuant to the
Fee Agreement as the councilman in Thomson was required to return
the amount he received for the sale of his property.

     It also is likely that the bond contracts would not be void
for the same reasons that the Redevelopment Contracts would not be
void. The Firm was not a party to the bond contracts and voiding
these contracts would render hardship to the Agency and the third

3.   Is the Availability of Legal Recourse Affected
     by Any Statute of Limitations?

     If the facts establish that it is appropriate to file an
action to determine the validity of the Fee Agreement or another
bond counsel or special counsel legal services agreement, or
whether the fees thereunder should be paid or recovered, based upon
the above principles, then the question whether an action based on
§ 1090, et seq. would be barred in whole or in part by the statute
of limitations must be addressed.  We addressed this question in a
previous opinion to you as follows.

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 16


     The Government Code does not specify a statute of limitations
applicable to § 1090, et seq., nor is there any case law on the
subject. Accordingly, general principles pertaining to determining
the applicable statute of limitations must be applied.

     The applicable statute of limitations is determined by the
nature of the right sued upon. Hedlund v. Superior Court of Orange
County, 34 Cal.3d 695, 701, 194 Cal.Rptr. 805, 809 (1983). On its
face, the liability that is created by §§ 1090 and 1092 appears to
be a liability created by statute. Accordingly, the applicable
statute of limitations for § 1090 would be three years under Code
of Civil Procedure § 338(a), pertaining to a liability, other than
a penalty or forfeiture, created by statute. Section 1092, on the
other hand, would appear to be governed by the one year statute of
limitations under Code of Civil Procedure § 340(1), which is
applicable to actions upon a statute for a penalty or forfeiture.
[See, County of San Diego v. Milotz, 46 Cal.2d, 761, 300 P.2d, 3-4
(1956); a penalty is "a sum of money made payable by way of punish-
ment for the non-performance of an act or for the performance of an
unlawful act"]

     Cases interpreting Code of Civil Procedure §§ 338(a) and
340(1) provide that these statutes are only applicable where the
liability could not exist but for the statute. The action must be
of a type which did not exist at common law. Winick Corporation v.
General Insurance Co. of America, 187 Cal.App.3d 142, 145, 231
Cal.Rptr. 606, 609 (1986); Aubry v. Goldhor, 201 Cal.App.3d 399,
404, 247 Cal.Rptr. 205, 207 (1988). The principle behind § 1090
was recognized at common law and therefore the liability under
§ 1090 would exist without the statute. Accordingly, Code of Civil
Procedure § 338(a) does not provide the applicable statute of
limitations for an action based on § 1090.

     A similar argument can be made with respect to § 1092. It was
recognized at common law that a contract void as against public
policy is entirely unenforceable. Section 1092 is the codification
of that principle with respect to contracts in violation of § 1090,
but it refers to such contracts as voidable, rather than void. The
statute itself does not provide for recovery of amounts paid under
the illegal contract. Rather, that is a common law extension and
interpretation of the statute. Accordingly, Code of Civil
Procedure § 340(1) does not apply.

     Since there is no other applicable statute of limitations, the
catch-all provision of Code of Civil Procedure § 343, which
provides for a four-year limitation period, applies. As a general
rule, a cause of action arises and the statute of limitations
begins to run when the wrongful act was committed. Myers v. East-

Luis A. Rodriguez
December 7, 1990
Page 17


wood Care Center, Inc., 31 Cal.3d 628, 634, 183 Cal.Rptr. 386, 389
(1982).

     Section 343 states in full as follows:

          Actions for relief not hereinbefore provided for.
     An action for relief not hereinbefore provided for must
     be commenced within four years after the cause of action
     shall have accrued.

     The earliest time that the cause of action here would accrue
would be the date when the Fee Agreement, or with respect to any
earlier contingency fee bond counsel agreements, those agreements,
were entered into by the Agency. However, this could vary signifi-
cantly depending upon the circumstances of each bond counsel fee
agreement and the timing of any Redevelopment Contracts related to
them which the Firm participated in as special counsel. The
question would be, when did the prohibited conflict arise, which
could vary with the facts of each bond issue and redevelopment
program.

     It should be noted that in certain circumstances, an action
questioning the validity of bond documents and/or some Redevelop-
ment Contracts, may be barred by a statute of limitations of only
sixty days from when the contract was entered into. Code of Civil
Procedure §§ 860 and 863.

   Under 46 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 74 (1965) (the "1965 OCAG"), if the

giving advice to the Agency as special counsel with respect to
matters affecting the issuance of any of the bonds provided for

ment by the Firm likely would violate § 1090. In the 1965 OCAG, a
city council was asked to approve a proposal to form an improvement
district and to issue tax-exempt bonds in connection therewith. A
city attorney who provided legal advice to the city council,
questioned whether he could be employed by and receive fees from

bond counsel for the improvement bond issue. The Attorney General
in the 1965 OCAG opined that this resulted in a conflict. The
Attorney General reasoned that since the city attorney's advice was
sought by the city council, the city attorney as a public officer
in an advisory position to the city council may not acquire a
financial interest in a contract which was made by the city
council.

   Although the special bond counsel in the 1965 OCAG was said
to be representing third parties who were proponents of issuing the
bonds, arguably § 1090 may be violated pursuant to the reasoning of
the 1965 OCAG even if the bond counsel did not represent the
interests of third parties. The Attorney General in the 1965 OCAG

applicable to actions upon a statute for a penalty or forfeiture.
lsee, County of san Diego v. Milotz, 46 CaI.2d, 761 ,3OO p.2d,3-4

ceneral Insurance Co. of Anerica, 187 Cal.App.3d L42, L45, 231_

under
